title: This Budget-Friendly RGB MiniLED TV Makes Video Games Look Real
gist: Hisense's UR9 is the first RGB Mini-LED TV to market at $2,000 for 65 inches, undercutting Samsung, Sony, and LG while delivering near-OLED picture quality and standout PC-gaming specs, signaling that the emerging RGB Mini-LED display category is arriving first through value-priced Chinese brands.

insights.claim: RGB Mini-LED TVs reached market in 2026 with Hisense's UR9 arriving first, followed by Sony's Bravia 7 Mark II and TCL's RM9L, establishing a new premium display category above OLED and QLED.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: A new display-tech S-curve is opening; watch which panel makers and brands capture the early premium-mainstream segment before it commoditizes.
insights.quote: RGB Mini-LED TVs have officially arrived, and Hisense's UR9 was the first to hit the market, followed by Sony's Bravia 7 Mark II and TCL’s RM9L.
insights.locator: RGB Mini-LED TVs have officially arrived

insights.claim: RGB Mini-LED emits discrete red, green, and blue lights rather than shining white or blue LEDs through an LCD panel as traditional LED and QLED sets do, yielding better color accuracy, contrast, brightness, and finer control over color zones.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: The mechanism is the moat: direct RGB backlighting is the technical basis for the category's picture-quality edge and a new BOM/supply-chain requirement for panel and LED suppliers.
insights.quote: The newer mini RGB works by emitting red, green, and blue lights, resulting in better color accuracy, excellent contrast and brightness, and finer control over color zones.
insights.locator: the general function of mini RGB tech

insights.claim: LG and Samsung are marketing a competing 'micro RGB' technology with smaller LEDs that they claim is more advanced than mini RGB, yet the reviewer says both approaches achieve roughly the same result.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: If micro vs mini RGB is largely marketing differentiation, incumbents' premium pricing on 'micro' may not hold against cheaper 'mini' entrants.
insights.quote: LG and Samsung use new tech called micro RGB, claiming it to be more advanced than mini RGB thanks to smaller LEDs, although both achieve roughly the same result.
insights.locator: LG and Samsung use new tech called micro RGB

insights.claim: The Hisense UR9, the brand's flagship, is priced at just $2,000 for the 65-inch model, materially undercutting flagship sets from Samsung, Sony, and LG.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Hisense is using aggressive flagship pricing to grab share in the emerging RGB Mini-LED tier, pressuring incumbents' premium margins.
insights.quote: The UR9 is the flagship in Hisense’s lineup, but it isn’t priced that way at just $2,000 for the 65-inch model I tested.
insights.locator: The UR9 is the flagship in Hisense’s lineup

insights.claim: Hisense entry-level TVs historically had poor contrast and brightness comparable to $800 bargain sets from TCL, Roku, and Amazon Fire TV, making the UR9's picture quality a notable break from the brand's budget reputation.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: A budget brand moving up-market on quality signals margin-mix improvement and a credibility challenge to premium incumbents.
insights.quote: including a few that had poor contrast and brightness, putting them more in line with TCL, Roku, and Amazon Fire TV bargain models that cost around $800
insights.locator: I’ve tested countless Hisense entry-level models

insights.claim: 65-inch OLED televisions are hovering around $2,700 and falling, but the $2,000 UR9 is positioned as reasonably priced for a Mini-RGB set relative to OLED.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: RGB Mini-LED is entering below OLED price points even at launch, threatening OLED's premium positioning as OLED itself deflates.
insights.quote: OLED televisions are coming down in price, hovering around $2,700 for a 65-inch.
insights.locator: OLED televisions are coming down in price

insights.claim: Mini RGB targets around 5,000 nits of brightness versus roughly 1,000 nits for OLED, while OLED retains an edge in deep blacks for immersive nighttime scenes.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: The brightness gap is the core selling point of RGB Mini-LED against OLED; black-level parity is the remaining gap to close.
insights.quote: a higher brightness rating (usually 5,000 nits compared to around 1,000 nits for OLED)
insights.locator: here’s what I’d expect from one, compared to that of an OLED

insights.claim: The UR9 meets 100 percent of the BT.2020 color gamut standard, a benchmark for wide-gamut HDR color reproduction.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Full BT.2020 coverage at $2,000 raises the color-performance floor for the price tier.
insights.quote: The UR9 meets 100 percent of the BT.2020 color gamut standard range.
insights.locator: The UR9 meets 100 percent of the BT.2020

insights.claim: On a 4K stream of Project Hail Mary the far more expensive LG Micro RGB Evo rendered the same scene at about the same level of vividness as the UR9, suggesting diminishing returns from paying up for micro RGB.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: If cheaper mini RGB matches pricier micro RGB output, the premium price umbrella over incumbents is fragile.
insights.quote: The much more expensive LG Micro RGB Evo rendered that scene at about the same level of vividness.
insights.locator: there’s a case to be made for mini RGB

insights.claim: The UR9 supports a 180-Hz refresh rate, rising to 330-Hz variable when a high-end PC connects via a side DisplayPort using a USB-C 40-Gbps cable, making it the reviewer's top RGB Mini-LED pick for PC gaming.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Adding a DisplayPort and high-refresh gaming spec targets the PC-gamer segment that TV incumbents underserve, a differentiator worth tracking.
insights.quote: its unusual-but-welcome 180-Hz refresh rate (330-Hz variable) when you link a high-end computer to the DisplayPort connection on the side
insights.locator: Not the case with this gaming monster

insights.claim: The UR9 ships with three HDMI 2.1 ports (versus the common two), a side DisplayPort, Ethernet, optical audio, coaxial, and two USB ports, but omits a 3.5-mm headphone jack.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Superior I/O at the price point is part of the value pitch aimed at multi-device gaming households.
insights.quote: The back of the TV features three HDMI 2.1 ports—an upgrade from many televisions that only have two.
insights.locator: The back of the TV features three HDMI 2.1 ports

insights.claim: Testing with an Alienware 16X Aurora laptop on an Nvidia GeForce RTX card at 330-Hz VRR produced immediate, low-latency response in titles like Crimson Desert, Forza Horizon 6, and 007 First Light.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: High-refresh TV+GPU pairing validates a converging PC-gaming/living-room use case that expands the addressable market for premium GPUs and displays.
insights.locator: I tested the UR9 with an Alienware 16X Aurora gaming laptop

insights.claim: The UR9 runs Google TV over Wi-Fi 6E but setup hit a known bug in the Google Home app's QR code that forced manual entry of Gmail credentials.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Onboarding friction on the Google TV stack is a recurring soft spot that affects the ecosystem, not just Hisense.
insights.locator: setting up the Google TV operating system was simple

insights.claim: The UR9's picture-quality settings are hit or miss and its remote is overly complex, and the higher-end LG Micro RGB Evo offers far more picture customizations.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Software/customization depth remains a premium-brand advantage that Hisense's hardware pricing has not yet neutralized.
insights.quote: The picture quality settings were less impactful on the UR9 than on the LG Micro RGB Evo, which is higher-end and has far more customizations.
insights.locator: The Hisense UR9 RGB MiniLED stands out
